name: Loredana Ifrim
cooked:
Are you ready to build the future of agentic automation on a global stage? UiPath AgentHack is here, and $50,000 in prizes is waiting for you!

The UiPath Community is inviting developers, automation engineers, data scientists, AI/ML specialists, industry professionals, and students worldwide to compete in our biggest hackathon yet. Over 7 weeks, you’ll design and ship a real, working agentic solution on the UiPath Platform, not a concept, not a slide deck, but a solution that handles complexity, survives interruptions, keeps humans in the loop, and solves something that matters.

What’s the challenge?

Choose a real-world business problem and build a working agentic solution on the UiPath Platform that addresses it. Your solution must fall under one of three challenge tracks, use UiPath as the orchestration and governance layer, and run on UiPath Automation Cloud. External frameworks like LangChain, CrewAI, and AutoGen are welcome, in fact, we encourage it.

How to join?

Go solo or form a team of up to 4 people. Register on Devpost, apply for your UiPath Labs sandbox environment, and start building. UiPath Labs comes fully equipped with agentic and AI units so you can build without constraints.

Register on Devpost

Apply for UiPath Labs access


The three challenge tracks:

Track 1: UiPath Maestro Case. Build a solution that orchestrates dynamic, exception-heavy business processes using UiPath case management capabilities. Your solution should move work through stages, involve handoffs between agents, robots, and people, and keep humans in charge at key decision points.

Think about scenarios like: insurance claims processing where cases move through intake, investigation, and settlement stages; patient care coordination where agents manage referrals, scheduling, and follow-ups across providers; or loan processing where agents collect documents, run compliance checks, and route exceptions to underwriters for human review.

Track 2: UiPath Maestro BPMN. Build a solution that models and runs an end-to-end business process using BPMN 2.0 in UiPath Maestro. Your process should orchestrate humans, robots, agents, and APIs through a defined flow with clear tasks, decisions, and handoffs.

Think about scenarios like: an invoice approval workflow where extraction agents process invoices, validation agents check compliance, robots post entries to ERP, and finance teams review exceptions; or a procure-to-pay process where agents parse requisition intent, route approvals based on budget rules, and an invoice agent reconciles discrepancies before escalating true exceptions to AP.

Track 3: UiPath Test Cloud. Create agents that use UiPath Test Cloud to reimagine how software testing is designed, automated, executed, and managed across modern enterprise environments. Build agents that evaluate requirements and turn them into meaningful test scenarios, identify fragile or outdated tests before they slow down a release, recommend fixes when automation breaks, or orchestrate the right tests at the right time based on risk, coverage, and change impact.

Think about: an agent, or broader agentic workflow, that analyzes results from large sets of automated test runs; groups failed tests into assertion errors (pointing to actual defects in the software under test), automation issues, and environment issues; and then handles those findings accordingly, such as escalating environment issues, suggesting and/or applying fixes for automation issues, or filing defects in Jira for assertion errors.

Bonus points Solutions that use coding agents through UiPath for Coding Agents, Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, or Gemini CLI, will receive additional points during judging. We’re especially interested in seeing how participants combine coding agents with low-code components, or blend UiPath-native agents with external agents, to solve complex problems.

Prizes: $50,000 in cash


Important dates

May 15, 2026 — Registration & submissions open

June 29, 2026 — Submission deadline

July 15, 2026 — Finalists announced

July 23, 2026 — Live finale

August 4, 2026 — Winners announced


Support throughout the hackathon

Throughout the hacking period, UiPath will host office hours with engineers and product experts, community meetups, and enablement sessions to help you get up to speed and refine your solution.

Who can participate?

Anyone with a passion for agentic automation and AI, automation professionals, data scientists, AI/ML specialists, business analysts, solution architects, enterprise customers, partners, students, software developers, and engineers. Individuals and teams of up to 4 are welcome.

This is your chance to help shape the next generation of enterprise agentic automation. Build something real. Solve something that matters. See you at UiPath AgentHack!

Help us spread the word, share this post and tag someone who should be building with us!

name: Andreea Tomescu
cooked:
AgentHack 2026 — Enablement Session #1: Q&A Summary

Thank you to everyone who joined our first AgentHack enablement session! The recording and presentation deck will be available here.

Below is a summary of all questions raised during the session, grouped by topic. We’ll keep this thread updated as more answers come in.

Two more sessions are coming up in the AMER timezone and more for EMEA/APJ timezones as well, don’t miss them:

  • June 9 — Winner Overview and Best Practices
  • June 15 — Office Hours for Questions and Support

We will put the links for the other enablements sessions in this thread whenever we have them live.

Track Selection

Q: For a solution that includes both structured BPMN automation and Maestro Case escalation — should it be submitted under Track 1 (Maestro Case) or Track 2 (Maestro BPMN)? → You can choose the track that covers best your solution.

Submission & Judging

Q: What does the finale consist of, do you rebuild your solution live in front of judges, or present it? Finalists present their working solution live on Zoom to a judging panel and take questions. You are not expected to rebuild anything, it’s a presentation and demo of what you’ve already built. Finalists can make changes in their solutions between 15th of June and 22nd of July.

Q: Who retains the IP rights to submitted code, the developer or UiPath? You retain full IP rights to your submission. UiPath receives only a non-exclusive license to use it for judging and promotional purposes. One thing to keep in mind: submissions require a public GitHub repo with an open-source license (MIT or Apache 2.0), so the version you submit will be publicly visible. Nothing in the rules prevents you from deploying a separate proprietary version in a real-world context after the hackathon.

Q: How do coding agents (e.g. Claude Code) need to be demonstrated to qualify for bonus points, in the demo video, the GitHub repo, or both? To qualify for bonus points, you need to document in your Devpost project description or GitHub README: (a) which coding agent tool was used, (b) how it contributed to the solution, and (c) at least one form of verifiable evidence — such as a prompt log, screenshots of the interaction, or a dedicated README section. The more thorough your documentation, the better your chances of receiving the full 2-point bonus.

Technical — Maestro

Q: Is there a recommended way to handle retry logic for failed steps within a Maestro BPMN flow? The execution trail will show a “Retried” badge, so screenshots and video will help document this. You can also configure retries directly, see the element-level retries section in the Maestro properties panel docs.

Q: I want to use a Gmail event trigger to start a Maestro Case process, but only for emails related to a specific use case (e.g. loan applications) — how do I avoid unnecessary instances being created? You can add filters to Maestro triggers directly from the properties panel — this should handle most filtering without needing a separate RPA step. If the subject line is unpredictable but the content is contextually relevant, one option is to use an RPA step upstream to evaluate the email semantically before invoking Maestro.

Q: Where can we find documentation for Maestro Case and Coded Apps? Coded Apps is now GA. Here are the relevant docs:

  • Release Notes
  • User Guide
  • TypeScript SDK
  • Case Management docs *(Tyler shared the direct link during the session, you can also see it here)
    Maestro Case Management Private Preview Guide-2026052615145726 (1).pdf (2.2 MB)

Q: We already use Copilot / Claude Code to automate things. Why do we need UiPath? Coding agents are good at generating code, but what they can’t do is manage what happens in production: retries when an API fails, audit trails for compliance, orchestration across SAP, Salesforce, and a legacy desktop app simultaneously. That’s what UiPath provides. The coding agent writes the script and UiPath makes it run at enterprise scale.

Platform & Tools

Q: Can external observability tools (e.g. Arize Phoenix) be used alongside UiPath Agent Builder, or should everything stay within the UiPath ecosystem? → As long as the solution uses UiPath as the execution and orchestration layer, yes.

Q: For RPA browser automation against SaaS tools — is UiPath Studio Web sufficient, or is Studio Desktop needed? → UiPath Studio Web is sufficient.

Q: Do we need to access the Coding Agents API independently, or is there a form or process through UiPath to get access? → You can access and use them independently.

We also shared the following resources in the chat:

Screenshot 2026-05-26 at 10.54.33 AM1920×1053 345 KB

Image202605261746201954×1090 207 KB

Support & Access

If you are experiencing issues accessing the UiPath Labs environment, please reach out directly to the team via the forum thread or email (andreea.tomescu@uipath.com). Individual access issues are being handled case by case.

Have more questions? Drop them in this thread and we’ll answer them here so everyone can benefit.

Hi! Since you have org admin access, you should be able to sort both of these yourself:

For the unattended license:

  1. Go to your org and open the Admin panel
  2. Navigate to Licenses
  3. Check if Unattended Robot units are available at the org level and allocate them to your folder

For IXP:

  1. In the Admin panel, go to External Applications or Integration settings
  2. Make sure IXP is enabled at the tenant level

If you see 0 units or the options aren’t there at all, ping us back and we’ll look into getting them provisioned for your tenant.

Let us know how it goes!

name: Nick Sudh
cooked:
If anyone is struggling with packing and publishing Maestro flow using uip CLI 1.195.0 (the latest version), it’s a bug and hopefully UiPath folks can resolve it soon.

Publishing the solution to the tenant solution feed fails on both paths:

  1. Studio Web → “Pack and upgrade”: packaging succeeds; the Publish package step then fails at “Uploading package to feed repository” with “An error has occurred! Please try again later.” Reproduced on versions 1.0.28 and 1.0.29.
  2. uip CLI (uip solution publish , CLI v1.195.0): fails with HTTP 400: “Invalid package folder structure. No root solution folder was found.”

Root cause I isolated: The current tooling - uip CLI 1.195.0, all @uipath/solution-tool versions (0.9.1–1.195), and Studio Web - emits the “unified” solution‑package format: packageInfo = {“version”:“v1”,“type”:“unified”} with a split
The tenant’s solution feed appears to reject the unified format.

Please enable unified‑solution‑package support on this tenant’s solution feed (or advise the correct way to publish a unified‑format solution to this tenant). No current CLI/Studio Web tooling produces the legacy format the feed accepts, so we cannot publish any new solution version.

name: Michi Yamamoto
cooked:
Hi team! AgentHack participant here, building a Track 1 (Maestro Case) solution.

My UiPath Labs sandbox (org hackathon26_738, DefaultTenant) has Maestro fully enabled (Flow, Case, and BPMN all respond), but the Actions / Action Center service doesn’t appear to be provisioned. Any Action Center call fails with a redirect to portal_/unregistered?serviceType=actions — for example:

uip tasks list --output json → Server returned non-JSON response (200 .../portal_/unregistered?serviceType=actions...)

This blocks the human-in-the-loop step in my case flow, since a Maestro User task surfaces as an Action Center task and pauses until a human completes it.

Could you enable the Actions / Action Center service on this tenant? Or, if participants are expected to self-provision it, point me to the right place in the Admin area. Happy to share more detail if useful. Thanks!

name: Nick Sudh
cooked:
Go to Admin->Tenant=>Add Services and enable Actions. Then assign the necessary licenses to your user. You are the org admin, so you can configure stuff yourself.

image1381×787 90.6 KB

name: Michi Yamamoto
cooked:
Hi folks, quick one for anyone who’s already gotten a Maestro Case to run on the hackathon tenant. What actually makes the serverless runtime kick in?

My case deploys and starts fine, but then it just sits at Pending forever and never runs a single step. Feels like nothing is picking it up to execute.

What I’m seeing:

  • The Orchestrator job goes to Running (RuntimeType: CaseManagement) but with no host machine
  • The Maestro instance never actually starts. 0 elements executed, no incidents, no error
  • sessions unattended list shows 0 sessions. So it looks like no serverless robot ever connects
  • Same thing every time (tried ~5 runs)

Stuff I’ve already checked so it’s probably not the obvious things:

  • Platform Units allocated (1,000), and Maestro + Serverless show up as consumers, 0 consumed
  • Unattended runtime allocated, Default Serverless machine licensed for it
  • That serverless machine is assigned to my folder (Shared + my solution folder)
  • Case validates clean, package deployed + activated fine

So before I go bug support, is there a setup step I’m missing to get serverless/Maestro execution going on these tenants, or is it just not enabled for us and an organizer needs to flip something? Anyone run into this?

(For organizers if it’s a tenant thing — Support ID 0362-9887-7873-9606.)

name: Nick Sudh
cooked:
This almost certainly isn’t a serverless/licensing problem on your end, your setup (platform units, unattended runtime, Default Serverless assigned to the folder) sounds correct. The tell is “0 elements executed.” That means the case instance was created but the orchestrator never fired the first element, so it’s not “no robot picked it up,” it’s “nothing told the case to start walking.” Serverless robots only get consumed once you reach an agent/process task inside a stage; you never get that far, so “0 sessions” is a symptom, not the cause.

What bit me with the exact same symptoms: a missing mainline start event in the compiled case definition. A Maestro Case needs a bpmn:startEvent (a message start, it carries a bpmn:messageEventDefinition/) in the compiled caseplan.json.bpmn, wired by a sequence flow into your first node (the initial-variables/setup node). That start event is what the case-entered entry condition fires against to kick off stage 1. If it’s absent, you get exactly what you’re describing:

  • job goes Running with RuntimeType: CaseManagement, no host, no incident, no error, 0 elements, sits forever.

Why it goes missing: the Studio Web canvas tends to drop the mainline start event when you round-trip the case (edit → re-save/download → re-pack).

How to confirm/fix:

  • Open the compiled caseplan.json.bpmn (not caseplan.json) and check there’s a <bpmn:startEvent …> with a bpmn:messageEventDefinition/ and an outgoing bpmn:sequenceFlow into your first node. If it’s not there, that’s it.
  • If you authored/edited in the canvas, re-verify it survived the last save, re-add the message start event and the flow into the first node, then re-pack/redeploy. (I keep a tiny
    merge script that re-applies the start event after every canvas download precisely because the canvas keeps dropping it.)
  • Sanity note: a bare jobs start will auto-walk the case fine once the start event exists.

Once it actually starts walking, watch your Serverless consumption tick up when it hits the first agent task, that part of your config is fine. If the start event is present and it
still parks at 0 elements, then it’s worth pinging an organizer, but check this first, it’s the much more common cause and produces this identical “looks like serverless never connects” picture.

name: Shivani Kaushik
cooked: Thank you for the clarification.

name: Michi Yamamoto
cooked:
Thanks, this is super helpful

It turned out that the mainline start event is actually present and wired in my compiled caseplan.json.bpmn:

<bpmn:startEvent id="trigger_1" name="Start Opportunity Case">

  ...

<bpmn:outgoing>edge_trigger_1-CaseInitialVariablesSetupNode</bpmn:outgoing>

<bpmn:messageEventDefinition/>

</bpmn:startEvent>

<bpmn:sequenceFlow id="edge_trigger_1-CaseInitialVariablesSetupNode"

sourceRef="trigger_1" targetRef="CaseInitialVariablesSetupNode"/>

(I authored the case as JSON via the uip CLI and packed with it — never round-tripped through the Studio Web canvas, so nothing got the chance to drop the start event.)

And it still parks at 0 elements. One detail that might differ from your case: on my side the Maestro instance itself never leaves Pending — StartedTimeUtc stays 0001-01-01, and sessions unattended list returns 0. The Orchestrator job shows Running (RuntimeType: CaseManagement) but with no host. So it looks less like “the engine fired and the definition broke” and more like the CaseManagement runtime never actually provisions/attaches in the first place.

Should I ping the organizer (nveenhof@gitlab.com), or is there another runtime-side thing worth checking before I escalate?

name: Nick Sudh
cooked:
1. If your start event has no bpmn:extensionElements/uipath:entryPointId, the engine has no trigger to attach to → it provisions the job shell (RuntimeType CaseManagement, no host) and parks forever. The “0 elements / StartedTimeUtc 0001-01-01 / 0 unattended sessions” combo is exactly what an unbound entry point looks like.

  2. That entryPointId GUID must be registered in entry-points.json. The CLI frequently emits an empty/partial entry-points file. If the start event references an entry point that isn’t in entry-points.json (or it’s empty), same result, nothing to bind. Make the GUID in the start event and the one registered match.

  3. The honest gotcha: the runtime-valid compiled .bpmn really only comes out of the Studio Web canvas. This is the one that bit me hardest. uip … pack does not truly compile your caseplan.json into a runtime-executable definition… it’ll happily carry whatever .bpmn is in the folder, but a hand/CLI-produced .bpmn can be structurally present and still not be a definition the engine will walk. We never got a CLI-only case to execute; the thing that made it run was importing the solution into Studio Web once, opening the case canvas, and Publishing (that’s what compiles the .bpmn and registers the entry-point trigger), then deploying that artifact. Ironically the canvas’s bad habit is dropping the start event on round-trip, so if you do this, re-verify the start event survived the publish.

  4. Root metadata flags. Make sure the case root has publishVersion: 2, intsvcActivityConfig: “v2”, caseAppEnabled: true (with a caseAppConfig), and the unified-schema flag set. Missing these has blocked clean provisioning for us. Also: the case project must be flat, not nested under a content/ dir.

So, not an organizer/licensing thing (Maestro Case execution definitely works on this tenant; consumption only ticks up once it hits the first agent task, which lines up with what you’re seeing). The most likely fix is: get a canvas-Published compiled .bpmn with a real uipath:entryPointId on the start event that matches a registered entry point. If you confirm the extensionElements + entry-points binding is present and it still parks at 0 elements after a canvas publish, that’s the point where pinging an organizer is justified.

name: Satish Prasad
cooked:
Screenshot 2026-06-26 124811423×868 48.6 KB

There are certain updated being made and now its going boneker

Bottom Line

The panel moving from right to left is not the problem. Autopilot does not know when it is stuck, does not say so, and does not escalate. Two real sessions, documented below.

Issue 1 — Silent loop on failed CLI discovery

Autopilot needed DownloadAttachmentByID metadata to wire a workflow correctly. It ran uip is resources describe three times with slightly different flags, got truncated output every time, told the user nothing, and the session ended with the third attempt frozen at "tool call executing, but not yet finished". The workflow was never delivered.

Fix needed: After two failed attempts at the same discovery command, surface it to the user and offer a path forward. Do not retry silently.

Issue 2 — Parameter mismatches authored silently, declared correct

Autopilot authored bucketName, folderPath, fileContent into an agent prompt. The actual API Workflow expected bucketId, folderId, fileContentBase64, mimeType. The agent ran, attachments silently failed at runtime, and the user only found out through testing. Autopilot had already shown a verification table with checkmarks declaring the schema correct.

Fix needed: Verify parameter names against the actual workflow input schema before authoring them into a prompt. Never show a verification table for something that has not been semantically confirmed.

Issue 3 — “File read back” is not the same as “file is correct”

After editing agent.json, Autopilot read the file, found the expected keys present, and declared everything verified. The contentTokens wiring was wrong — variables in inputSchema were not correctly referenced in the prompt blocks. The user re-diagnosed a problem Autopilot had already marked resolved.

Fix needed: Semantic checks, not structural. Confirm every inputSchema variable is referenced in the prompt with correct syntax. Key presence ≠ correct wiring.

Issue 4 — No recovery when a session hangs or fails

When the session froze on the pending tool call, there was no timeout, no partial summary, no handoff. The user lost the entire session with no record of what had been completed.

Fix needed: Tool call timeout with graceful degradation. Always produce a “completed / in progress / blocked” summary before a session exits in any state.

Issue 5 — No progress visibility in long sessions

Both sessions ran 40–50+ tool calls with zero unprompted status updates. The user had to read individual tool call details to understand where things stood.

Fix needed: Proactive checkpoint after ~15 tool calls: what is done, what is in progress, what is blocked.

Summary Table

Situation Current Expected
CLI returns empty output twice Retry silently Escalate to user
Parameter names unverified Author assumed names Verify schema first
File edited and read back Declare verified on key presence Semantic wiring check
Tool call hangs Session freezes Timeout + partial summary
Session > 15 tool calls No updates Progress checkpoint

name: Nick Sudh
cooked:
I fixed it in my project. I was getting the same error. It seems that the latest CLI has issues.

Steps I did.

  1. Confirm it’s the format bug. Unzip your published/packed solution and check packageInfo.json:
    • {“version”:“v1”,“type”:“unified”} + a resources/ directory = unified (rejected).
    • {“version”:“v1”} (no type) + a single resources.json file + configurations/default/configuration.json + files//*.nupkg = legacy (accepted).
    1. Get a legacy-format baseline. Download the last version you successfully published, it’s already legacy:
      uip solution packages download -d ./legacy
    2. If you’ve never published successfully, you’ll need a legacy package from any working publish to use as the template.
    3. Build the project(s) you changed with normal tooling and extract just that project’s .nupkg (the whole-solution pack being unified doesn’t matter, you only need the inner per-project nupkg).
    4. Preserve the entry-point id. Re-packing regenerates the entry-point uniqueId in content/entry-points.json, which breaks the upgrade with Cannot set entry point … not found in version X. So build your new nupkg from the old project nupkg: replace only the content files you actually changed, keep the old content/entry-points.json, and bump the version in the .nuspec and the .psmdcp core-properties.
    5. Splice into the legacy package. Drop your corrected nupkg into the same files// folder (reuse the guid), then bump that one project’s version in:
    • resources.json — package key, and the file entry’s name, url, and version (the easy miss → “Required file dependency not found”),
    • configurations/default/configuration.json — fileName, resourceKey, package.key, packageVersion,
    • solutionMetadata.json — packageVersion + a fresh packageVersionKey GUID.
      Leave packageInfo.json as {“version”:“v1”}. Verify: no occurrence of the old version string anywhere near your project name.
    1. Publish the spliced legacy package.

name: Nick Sudh
cooked:
Here’s a friendly version - I asked Claude code to write a easy step-by-step guide:

We ran into this identical error (“No solution tool factory is registered”) and eventually root-caused it. It’s a server-side packaging failure in Studio Web - it usually is NOT your project being broken, so don’t panic. Work through these in order, cheapest first:

  1. Isolate an offending project (do this first).
    Publish each member project individually from its own editor. If one fails its own publish, that single project is the culprit - fix or remove it and re-add. The solution pack fails as a whole if any one member can’t pack.

  2. If every member publishes fine on its own but the solution still fails → it’s the platform packer, not you. That’s the case we were in. Two things strongly correlated with the failure for us:

    • A Maestro Flow (or Maestro Case) in the solution. If yours contains one, temporarily remove it, then try to publish the solution again. If it succeeds without the Maestro Flow, you’ve confirmed the trigger. (UiPath acknowledged this as a known bug on the forum and routed it to engineering, it’s a server-side pack/format issue, not your code.)
    • An outdated UiPath CLI if you’re packing locally - old versions couldn’t pack enriched Maestro Flow files.

  3. Try publishing via the CLI instead of the Studio Web “Publish” button. The browser publish uses a server-side snapshot packer (the thing that’s throwing). The CLI packs locally and often either works or gives a much clearer error:

    npm i -g @uipath/cli@latest # get off old versions
    uip login
    uip solution pack # produces a .zip
    uip solution publish <the.zip>

If the CLI publish instead says “No root solution folder was found”, that’s the same underlying platform bug (the tenant feed rejecting the package format) - see step 4.

  1. If you’ve published a working version before, you can ship a fix without the broken packer: download your last good published package (uip solution packages download -d ./pkg), swap in only the project you changed, bump that project’s version refs, re-zip, and uip solution publish it. This bypasses the snapshot packer entirely.

A few extra notes for anyone comfortable with the CLI:

  • The two errors are the same bug, two surfaces: Studio Web publish → “No solution tool factory is registered”; CLI publish → “No root solution folder was found”. Both are the tenant solution feed rejecting the package format the current tooling emits.
  • Removing the Maestro Flow did NOT fix it for us (the format is the same with or without it) - but it’s still worth testing, because for some people the Maestro Flow is what makes the local pack fail, which is a different, fixable problem from the feed rejection.
  • The legacy-format splice (step 4) is the only thing that reliably got a fixed package onto the feed for us, but it requires a prior good published version to download and swap into. No prior version = no splice; those folks are stuck on the platform bug and should escalate.

Looks like you do not have the required licenses to access Action Center

name: Nick Sudh
cooked:
Go to Admin-> click on your Tenant (generally Default Tenant)->Services->Add Service:

  • Select Actions from Add Services and click Add
  • Then go back to your Org (hackathon26_XXX)-Click on Accounts and local groups->Click on your name->Click Licenses and Assign yourself the Pro license. Pro license gives you “Automation Developer role”.
  • Now go to Orchestrator->“Your Project folder”->Home->Accounts-> Manage Access, and make yourself the folder admin
  • Now go to Actions, and you should be able to setup Catalogs under your project folder.
